Title: Resistance-Gene Directed Discovery of a Natural Product Herbicide with a New Mode of Action

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common BCAA core pathway to isoleucine, leucine, and valine
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step of the three-enzyme pathway described in the paper
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid (ketoisovalerate); α-keto acid precursors of BCAAs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes β-dehydration of dihydroxyacids to form α-keto acid precursors for branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential amino acids required for plant growth and survival.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms immediate α-keto acid precursors required for BCAA production
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast (when engineered for plant expression via fused chloroplast localization signal)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Biosynthetic gene cluster-associated in Aspergillus terreus
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Ubiquitin-10 promoter (constitutive) in Arabidopsis; TEF1 promoter (constitutive) in yeast assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Confers strong resistance to aspterric acid (AA) while maintaining DHAD catalytic function; transgenic Arabidopsis expressing astD remains largely unaffected by AA treatment.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assay; inhibitor assay; yeast complementation and AA-sensitivity assay; transgenic expression in Arabidopsis with metabolically relevant herbicide-resistance phenotype; western blot verification
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key essential pathway step; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: AA treatment on agar media; AA spray application; ILV dropout conditions in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AstD is a cluster-encoded DHAD homolog from Aspergillus terreus that catalyzes the same β-dehydration reaction as housekeeping DHAD. 2. Unlike plant and fungal housekeeping DHADs, AstD is insensitive to aspterric acid even at high concentrations, validating it as the self-resistance enzyme. 3. Expression of astD in yeast and Arabidopsis confers strong resistance to AA, demonstrating direct utility as a herbicide-resistance gene.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising transgene for engineering AA-resistant crops; can also guide endogenous DHAD editing to create resistance alleles.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: AstD has much lower catalytic turnover than housekeeping DHAD, which could constrain performance depending on expression level.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AstD is ~70% similar to fungal housekeeping DHAD and its AA insensitivity is explained by a more constricted hydrophobic entrance to the catalytic chamber inferred from modeling.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common BCAA core pathway to isoleucine, leucine, and valine
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step of the three-enzyme pathway described in the paper
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid (ketoisovalerate); α-keto acid precursors of BCAAs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes β-dehydration of dihydroxyisovalerate to ketoisovalerate, producing α-keto acid precursors for BCAA synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential amino acids required for plant growth and development.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms immediate α-keto acid precursors required for BCAA production
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-inferred from the chloroplast localization signal derived from Arabidopsis DHAD
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Endogenous promoter in wild type; CLS sequence also used in transgene design
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AA competitively inhibits Arabidopsis DHAD with submicromolar potency, thereby blocking BCAA biosynthesis and suppressing plant growth.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified enzyme assay; IC50 and Ki determination; crystal structure of holo-pDHAD (PDB 5ZE4); docking/modeling; Arabidopsis growth inhibition assays
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: critical essential pathway step; not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: AA exposure in vitro and in planta
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis DHAD is biochemically active on dihydroxyisovalerate and is potently inhibited by AA. 2. AA acts as a competitive inhibitor with Ki about 0.30 μM. 3. Structural analysis of holo-pDHAD supports the binding pocket features underlying AA inhibition and herbicidal activity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Endogenous DHAD is a candidate editing target to create AA-resistant crops by altering inhibitor-binding residues while preserving activity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because DHAD is essential for BCAA biosynthesis, disruptive editing would likely impair growth.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The holo-pDHAD structure includes a required 2Fe-2S cluster and Mg2+; the paper proposes resistance-conferring edits based on AstD-like pocket narrowing.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate to form the sesquiterpene skeleton (-)-daucane.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Natural product herbicide that inhibits DHAD and suppresses plant growth.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms the carbon skeleton precursor of the target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Within conserved ast biosynthetic gene cluster
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required to initiate aspterric acid biosynthesis by generating the daucane scaffold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; metabolite purification and structure elucidation by NMR/GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: committed step for AA scaffold formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. astA encodes the sesquiterpene cyclase that produces (-)-daucane. 2. This product is the first dedicated intermediate in aspterric acid biosynthesis. 3. Coexpression with downstream oxidases enables conversion toward AA.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for heterologous biosynthesis and production engineering of AA or analogs, rather than crop breeding directly.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified as one of four conserved genes in the ast cluster used for resistance-gene-directed natural product discovery.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes (-)-daucane to produce the α-epoxy carboxylate intermediate in AA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Natural product herbicide that inhibits DHAD and suppresses plant growth.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms an immediate oxidative intermediate en route to the target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Within conserved ast biosynthetic gene cluster
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts the AstA product into an oxidized intermediate required for AA production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stepwise heterologous expression in yeast; metabolite purification and NMR structure elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. astB is a cluster P450 required for conversion of (-)-daucane to compound 2. 2. This oxidation step is necessary for downstream formation of aspterric acid. 3. Coexpression data define astB as a core AA pathway enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for synthetic biology production of AA and analog discovery.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific oxidation chemistry was assigned from product structures generated by combinatorial expression.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Further oxidizes compound 2 at carbon 15 to yield an alcohol that undergoes intramolecular epoxide opening to form aspterric acid.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Natural product herbicide that inhibits DHAD and suppresses plant growth.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Within conserved ast biosynthetic gene cluster
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Completes aspterric acid biosynthesis from the AstB-derived intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stepwise heterologous expression in yeast; metabolite purification and NMR structure elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. astC is the downstream P450 that enables formation of aspterric acid. 2. Coexpression of astA, astB, and astC yields AA as the dominant product. 3. astC likely performs C15 oxidation that triggers intramolecular epoxide opening to produce AA.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for synthetic biology-based AA production and diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Terminal step assignment is inferred from pathway reconstruction in heterologous host.
